does: This paper constructs a comprehensive AI risk taxonomy by systematically analyzing eight government regulatory policies from the EU, US, and China alongside 16 corporate safety policies. The authors identify and organize 314 distinct risk categories into a four-level hierarchical framework: System & Operational Risks, Content Safety Risks, Societal Risks, and Legal & Rights Risks. The taxonomy serves as a bridge between how different sectors conceptualize and communicate AI hazards, explicitly mapping overlaps and divergences between public regulatory language and private industry terminology. This unified framework enables direct comparison of risk definitions across institutional contexts, revealing how similar concerns are framed differently across jurisdictions and whether certain risks receive asymmetric attention from governments versus companies.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI safety research that has emphasized the need for shared conceptual frameworks and taxonomies to advance the field. It responds to fragmentation in how AI risks are categorized across regulatory bodies and corporations, extending prior work on AI governance, safety benchmarks, and risk taxonomies by synthesizing diverse policy documents into a coherent structure. The paper connects to emerging literature on AI regulation, alignment research emphasizing interpretability and controllability, and work on fairness and bias in machine learning. It engages with international policy efforts like the EU AI Act and executive orders from multiple nations, while also acknowledging corporate safety initiatives from major AI developers. The comprehensive scope reflects growing recognition that AI safety requires coordinated institutional efforts rather than siloed approaches.
enables: This taxonomy provides infrastructure for future AI safety research, regulation, and industry practice by establishing a common vocabulary for risk discussion across sectors. It enables more efficient cross-sector information sharing about safety incidents, risk mitigation strategies, and emerging threats. Organizations can now benchmark their risk frameworks against this standard, identify gaps in their safety practices, and adopt proven mitigation approaches from other institutions. The unified framework supports development of standardized evaluation benchmarks and auditing procedures for generative AI systems. It creates a foundation for comparing how effectively different regulatory approaches address identified risks and for tracking whether policy-driven risk categories align with real-world harms. Future work can use this taxonomy to design better testing protocols, develop automated risk assessment tools, and facilitate international cooperation on AI governance.
